result.content: FINAL_ANSWER: 1. Represent both the initial tree and the final tree using adjacency lists. 2. Perform a post-order traversal on the initial tree to ensure that all merges occurring in subtrees are processed before the merges at the current node. 3. For each node u in the initial tree, maintain a list of its current children. 4. Identify the set of target children for node u in the final tree. 5. For each target child v of u in the final tree, identify which child (or group of children) from the initial tree maps to it. 6. Any children of u in the initial tree that are not explicitly listed as direct children of u in the final tree must be merged into one of the target children. 7. Specifically, for a node u, iterate through its initial children. If a child is not a direct child in the final tree, it must be merged into the first child in the initial list that is a direct child in the final tree (or the first available target child). 8. Use a queue to record each merge operation as a triplet (u, child_to_merge, target_child) in the order they are identified during the post-order traversal. 9. Output the sequence of operations stored in the queue. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that we resolve all structural changes within subtrees before addressing the parent nodes, which is necessary because a merge operation at a lower level changes the child structure that a higher-level node relies upon. By processing leaves-to-root, we maintain the integrity of the tree structure during the transformation sequence.
